children.name: Cinclosoma
children.description: The quail-thrushes — a genus of medium-sized, ground-dwelling passerines with a quail-like build, fine dark scalloping on the upperparts, and a distinctive white eyebrow stripe. They inhabit arid and semi-arid woodlands and shrublands across Australia, where they run rapidly through the undergrowth like small quail. They are secretive, usually detected by their fine, high-pitched calls.

children.name: Cinclosoma punctatum
children.description: The largest quail-thrush, with a striking black breast spot, a white throat, and finely spotted upperparts, found in the forests and woodlands of eastern Australia. It forages on the forest floor in a deliberate, thrush-like manner, scratching through leaf litter for insects. Its melancholy, piping whistle is far easier to hear than the bird itself is to see.

children.name: Cinclosoma castanotum
children.description: A strikingly patterned quail-thrush with a chestnut back, black-and-white face markings, and a bold white eyebrow, endemic to the mallee and dry woodlands of southern Australia. It is a shy, terrestrial bird that freezes when disturbed, relying on its cryptic plumage for camouflage. It runs swiftly through the understorey, rarely taking flight.

children.name: Ptilorrhoa
children.description: The jewel-babblers — a genus of brilliantly coloured, ground-dwelling passerines endemic to the rainforests of New Guinea. Their name is well earned: they are among the most vividly plumaged of all Australasian passerines, with combinations of deep blues, purples, chestnut, and white. They run and hop along the forest floor with a distinctive bobbing gait.

children.name: Ptilorrhoa leucosticta
children.description: A colourful ground bird with rich chestnut upperparts spotted with white, a blue-and-black head, and a white throat, found in the mid-montane forests of New Guinea. It forages on the forest floor for insects, flicking leaves aside with its bill in the manner of a thrush. Its strong, clear whistle carries through the mountain forests.

children.name: Ptilorrhoa caerulescens
children.description: A magnificent deep-blue ground bird with a black crown, white ear-patch, and a long, graduated tail, found in the lowland and hill rainforests of New Guinea. It moves through the dark forest understorey in a quiet, deliberate manner, feeding on insects and fallen fruit. Its song is a beautifully clear, haunting series of descending whistles.
